text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, two bilateral direct inguinal hernias are visible. The surgeon creates a flap by dissecting the peritoneum to access the layers of the abdominal wall, preparing to conceal the mesh from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon begins on the patient's left side, dissecting down to the pubic bone. The left-sided direct inguinal hernia is reduced and freed from surrounding attachments. The surgeon then reduces a small indirect hernia around the spermatic cord structures.

The surgeon moves to the right side to create a flap and reduce the direct inguinal hernia, pulling it out of the direct space. The direct hernia is completely reduced. Mesh is introduced into the abdominal cavity, positioned to cover all hernia spaces on both sides, overlapping across the pubic bone. Absorbable sutures are placed to fixate the mesh in position.

The final surgical view shows all hernias reduced with the mesh securely in place, covering the hernia spaces from side to side. The surgeon closes the flaps, ensuring the mesh is hidden from the abdominal cavity, preventing exposure to the underlying bowel.
The surgeon shows the completely closed flap, which isolates the mesh from the abdominal cavity. The sutures have been removed, leaving the mesh hidden beneath the abdominal wall.
